notes: Revelation names Antipas as Christ's faithful witness who was killed in Pergamum.
Jesus is seized in the passion narratives after Judas arrives with those who came to take him.
Luke-Acts narrates Jesus being taken up after the resurrection appearances.
Opening public-ministry event in the Gospel narratives.
Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus bury Jesus in a tomb after the crucifixion.
Calling of Simon, Andrew, James, and John in Galilee.
Frumentius consecrated bishop by Athanasius and returns to Aksum c. 328–333; King Ezana converts; Christianity becomes state religion of the Aksumite Empire.

Conversion of King Abgar VIII of Osroene to Christianity c. 200 AD, making Edessa likely the first Christian state; the historical kernel behind the later legendary Abgar-Jesus correspondence in Eusebius HE 1.13.

Church council convened by Emperor Constantine at Arles in Gaul, 314; addressed the Donatist controversy; attended by 3 British bishops (Eborius of York, Restitutus of London, Adelfius of Colchester/Lincoln); earliest firm attestation of organized British Christianity.

North African council convened under Cyprian c. 251 after the Decian persecution to establish policy on the readmission of the lapsed; distinguished categories of lapse and mandated graded penance under episcopal authority; opposed both the rigorist position (Novatian) and the lax granting of peace by confessors.

event_label: Council of Carthage (252) on Infant Baptism
notes: North African council convened under Cyprian c. 252 responding to Bishop Fidus's question about delaying infant baptism until the eighth day; the council unanimously ruled that baptism should not be delayed — infants may be baptized as early as the second or third day after birth; key witness for infant baptism as assumed standard practice.

Council held September 1, 256 under Cyprian's presidency with 87 North African bishops present; each bishop individually declared heretical baptism invalid and mandated rebaptism of converts from heretical groups; the fullest surviving example of North African collegial episcopal practice; opposed by Pope Stephen I of Rome.
Jerusalem meeting concerning Gentile inclusion and circumcision.
Execution of Jesus under Roman authority in Jerusalem.

Cyprian of Carthage was arrested, tried before the proconsul Galerius Maximus, and beheaded on September 14, 258 during the Valerianic persecution; the Acta Proconsularia preserve the trial proceedings; Pontius the Deacon wrote the earliest Latin Christian biography (Vita Cypriani) describing his death; Cyprian was the first African bishop to be martyred.

Empire-wide persecution under Emperor Decius c. 250–251; required all citizens to sacrifice to Roman gods and obtain a certificate (libellus); many Christians lapsed; triggered the controversy over readmission of the lapsed that led to the Novatian schism.
Imperial persecution of Christians and Jews under Emperor Domitian c. 95-96 CE; traditional context for Revelation and John's exile to Patmos; 1 Clement likely written in this setting.

Construction of the earliest archaeologically identified Christian house-church at Dura-Europos c. 232 AD; includes a baptistery with wall paintings depicting biblical scenes; destroyed when Sasanians besieged the city c. 256.
Emperor Jovian ceded Nisibis to Sasanian Persia by treaty in 363 after Julian's failed campaign; the Christian population including Ephrem the Syrian was forced to relocate, most moving to Edessa.
Fire that devastated much of Rome in 64 CE under Nero.

event_label: Herodian Persecution of the Jerusalem Church
notes: Persecution under Herod in Acts 12, including James's death and Peter's arrest.

Hippolytus set himself up as rival bishop of Rome c. 217 after the election of Callistus I, whom he accused of modalism and moral laxity; sometimes called the first antipope; the schism persisted through the pontificates of Urban I and Pontian until reconciliation in Sardinian exile c. 235.
Ignatius of Antioch is transported to Rome and martyred in the arena under Trajan c. 108-110.

event_label: Martyrdom of James son of Zebedee
notes: Execution of James the brother of John under Herod Agrippa I.
Josephus reports James the brother of Jesus being delivered to be stoned during the high priesthood of Ananus.
Great persecution against the Jerusalem church following Stephen's martyrdom.
Jesus is handed over to Pontius Pilate, examined, and condemned in the passion narrative.
Revelation situates John on the island called Patmos because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.
Herod Antipas imprisons and kills John, as narrated in Mark 6 and contextualized by Josephus.
Justin Martyr is condemned and beheaded in Rome c. 165 under the prefecture of Rusticus during the reign of Marcus Aurelius.
Jesus shares the final meal with his disciples before the passion and gives the bread and cup tradition.
Severe persecution of Christians at Lyons and Vienne in Gaul c. 177 AD under Marcus Aurelius; attested in a letter preserved by Eusebius; Irenaeus was sent as envoy to Rome during this crisis.
Marcion excommunicated from the Roman church c. 144 CE after his rejection of the OT God and construction of a reduced canon; he thereafter organized his own rival churches.
Execution of Alban, proto-martyr of Britain; date debated between the Severan persecution (c. 209) and the Diocletianic persecution (c. 304); earliest witness is Gildas (6th c.).

Pantaenus sent from Alexandria as far as India (c. 180) per Eusebius HE 5.10; reportedly found Christians using a Hebrew Gospel of Matthew left by Bartholomew; identity of 'India' debated (possibly south Arabia or actual India).
Emperor Constantius II sends bishop Theophilus the Indian c. 354 on a diplomatic-missionary embassy to Himyar (South Arabia); Theophilus builds churches in Zafar and Aden per Philostorgius HE 3.4–6.
Nero's punishment of Christians in Rome after the Great Fire, as narrated by Tacitus.

Novatian, a Roman presbyter, set himself up as rival bishop of Rome c. 251 in opposition to Pope Cornelius; refused readmission of Christians who had lapsed during the Decian persecution; condemned by a Roman synod; his followers formed the Novatianist sect which spread across the empire.

Bishop Demetrius of Alexandria convened two synods c. 231–232 condemning Origen's irregular ordination in Caesarea and expelling him from the Alexandrian church; Origen relocated permanently to Caesarea where he continued teaching and writing for over twenty years.

During the Decian persecution c. 250–251, Origen was imprisoned and subjected to prolonged torture including chains, the rack, and threats of fire; he survived but his health was broken and he died c. 253–254 in Tyre.
Encounter and reversal associated with the Damascus road and its aftermath.
Early Christian memory of Paul's suffering and death.
Outpouring of the Holy Spirit on the gathered disciples in Jerusalem.

Systematic persecution of Christians in the Sasanian Empire beginning c. 340 under Shapur II; motivated partly by Constantine's declaration of Rome as a Christian empire; Aphrahat wrote his later Demonstrations during this crisis; lasted intermittently until Shapur's death in 379.
Early Christian memory of Peter's suffering and death.
Polycarp bishop of Smyrna is burned and stabbed in the stadium at Smyrna c. 155-156 AD.

Dispute c. 190-195 between Victor of Rome and Asian churches led by Polycrates of Ephesus over whether Easter should be observed on 14 Nisan or the Sunday following; Irenaeus of Lyons mediated and persuaded Victor not to excommunicate the Asian churches.
Earliest Christian proclamation and Gospel narratives report that Jesus was raised after his death and burial.
Roman destruction of Jerusalem and its temple in 70 CE.

event_label: Sardinian Exile of Hippolytus and Pontian
notes: Bishop Pontian and the schismatic Hippolytus were both exiled to the mines of Sardinia in 235 under Emperor Maximinus Thrax; they were reconciled before death; their bodies were brought back to Rome by Pope Fabian c. 236.
Representative teaching scene in Galilee.
Early Jerusalem martyrdom narrated in Acts 7.

Synod convened c. 244 at Bostra in Arabia to address the monarchian views of Bishop Beryllus who denied Christ's pre-existent divinity; Origen was invited to debate Beryllus and successfully persuaded him to return to orthodox teaching per Eusebius HE 6.33.
Jerusalem confrontation during the final phase of ministry.
Jesus enters Jerusalem in a publicly acclaimed approach before the passion week.
